title: Himachal: Forest fires triple in 2 weeks amid heatwave conditions
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds") Himachal Pradesh
full_text:
Even as showers in several parts of Himachal Pradesh on Friday brought down temperatures and offered temporary relief from the intense summer heat, the prolonged spell of extreme weather has already taken a heavy toll on the state’s forests. Himachal has witnessed a sharp rise in summer forest fire incidents this season, with officials reporting a three-fold surge in less than a fortnight.
According to data available on the forest department’s fire reporting portal, the state has so far recorded 286 forest fire incidents this season, already surpassing last summer’s total of 276 fires, even though the official fire season is still over two weeks away from ending.
The scale of the increase has alarmed authorities. On May 17, Himachal had reported only 66 fire incidents. However, within less than two weeks, another 220 incidents were reported, pushing the total beyond the previous year’s figure. Nearly 30 incidents were reported during the last 24 hours alone.
Forest officials attributed the sudden spike to heatwave-like conditions prevailing across lower and mid-hill regions over the past several days. Dry pine needles carpeting forest floors, low moisture levels and gusty winds created ideal conditions for fires to ignite and spread rapidly across vast stretches of forest land.
Among the state’s 11 forest circles, the Mandi circle emerged as the worst affected in terms of the number of incidents. It recorded 92 forest fires this season, affecting nearly 536.16 hectares of forest area.
However, the maximum destruction in terms of area burnt was reported from the Shimla forest circle, where around 1,573.36 hectares of forest land have been ravaged by fires so far this season, making it the most severely damaged region in the state.
The Dharamsala forest circle witnessed 70 fire incidents, damaging nearly 247 hectares, while the Nahan circle reported 56 incidents affecting around 610 hectares of forest land.
Officials said forest staff have been working round the clock under difficult terrain and extreme weather conditions to contain the fires. The sudden rise in incidents has placed considerable pressure on the department’s manpower and resources.
The summer forest fire season in Himachal Pradesh officially runs from April 1 to June 15 every year, a period considered highly vulnerable due to rising temperatures and the accumulation of dry pine needles in forest areas.

title: PH Army: 2 trainees died of heat stroke, complications - GMA Network

full_text:

The two trainees who died during military training in Capas, Tarlac succumbed to complications from severe heat stroke amid extreme physical exertion, the Philippine Army Training Command (TRACOM) said Saturday.
In a statement, the training command said official medical certificates indicate the final diagnosis as brain complications and swelling (encephalopathy) as a result of severe heat stroke.
Secondary complications of heat stroke, the TRACOM said, may include changes in mental state or behavior, agitation, restlessness, delirium, seizures, coma, and multiple electrolyte imbalances.
The Philippine Army extended its condolences to the families of the deceased, identified as Candidate Soldiers (CS) Johndyl Lemosniro of New Corella, Davao del Norte, and Junel Flores of Lamitan City, Basilan.
The TRACOM earlier assured it would launch a "full and immediate investigation" into the incident, stressing that training is "inherently demanding, but it must always be conducted within established safety standards."
On Saturday, the Philippine Army said immediate medical intervention was provided to the trainees.
"Both individuals were rushed to the Camp O'Donnell Medical Treatment Facility (CODMTF). Because they were unconscious and in critical condition, medical staff immediately performed endotracheal intubation to secure their airways and assist their breathing," the statement read.
They were transferred to the Tarlac Provincial Hospital for laboratory testing and intensive treatment.
In the wake of the incident, the Philippine Army assured the public and the families of their recruits that "stringent safety policies, medical protocols, and precautionary procedures remain enforced." — VDV, GMA News
